questions.prompt: How should you handle asset performance labels (Best/Good/Low)?

choices.text: Replace Low assets weekly with new variants; let Best assets continue running. Best assets are doing the work — pausing them concedes delivery to weaker creative. Over time, new variants may surface as winners and rotate the labels — that's healthy evolution. Goal is fresh variety at the bottom of the library while top performers keep delivering. Aim for 1-2 Low asset replacements per asset group per week.
Pause Low assets only, don't add.

questions.explanation: Replace Low, keep Best. Refresh the bottom of the library weekly.

questions.prompt: Three asset groups within a campaign all converge to similar performance after 30 days. Why might that be a problem?

choices.text: Suggests the asset groups aren't meaningfully different — same audience signals, similar creative, overlapping search themes. PMax is treating them as equivalent and the segmentation isn't doing work. Either consolidate (one strong asset group instead of three weak ones with auction overlap) or differentiate more aggressively: different customer-match audiences per group, distinct creative angles, separated search themes. Asset groups separate intents the algorithm shouldn't blend; if they're blending anyway, the segmentation isn't earning its complexity.

questions.explanation: Convergence = asset groups not actually different. Consolidate or differentiate.
